petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-petitioning businessman, apparently of Filipino origin, who sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in business under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. He serves as president of three U.S. companies and two Filipino companies, and is a board member and international service director of a rotary club. The Texas Service Center denied the petition on March 15, 2011, and he appealed to the AAO on April 14, 2011.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed membership based on his position as international service director on the Board of Directors of a rotary club. Supporting documents included: (1) an unsigned certificate of membership dated April 19, 2010; (2) July 21, 2010 minutes of the rotary club's monthly board meeting showing the petitioner as international service director; (3) an article from asianjournal.com; (4) a July 2010 Rotary Club newsletter; (5) photographs of the petitioner with unnamed individuals; (6) an August 5, 2010 letter from an officer of the rotary club; (7) an August 4, 2010 online printout from rotary.org about the guiding principles of a rotary club; and (8) an August 5, 2010 Wikipedia printout entitled 'Rotary International.' Petitioner had also submitted evidence of memberships in other organizations at the initial stage but did not pursue them on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed this criterion for multiple reasons. First, the petitioner provided no evidence such as membership requirements or bylaws indicating what qualifications an applicant must meet to become a member. Second, even if such documents had been provided, the petitioner failed to establish that membership requirements include outstanding achievements as judged by recognized national or international experts. Third, the petitioner did not establish through objective evidence that he is a member at all — the April 2010 certificate of membership was unsigned and thus facially deficient and given no evidential weight; while other evidence indicated he is a board member, the plain language of the criterion requires membership in an association, not a board membership of a rotary club. Fourth, the plain language of the criterion requires memberships in associations (plural), and even if the rotary club involvement counted as one, petitioner had not shown a second qualifying association. Finally, the petitioner abandoned claims regarding other organizations by failing to raise them on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) an undated article published in Woman, a weekly magazine in the Philippine Post, about the petitioner's business accomplishments; (2) a copy of the table of contents of Woman listing the article; (3) an August 4, 2010 online printout about Manila Bulletin Publishing Corporation; (4) an August 4, 2010 online printout about Manila Standard Today; and (5) the petitioner's curriculum vitae listing articles under 'Recognitions.' Counsel had also previously asserted publication of articles in Entrepreneur Magazine, Manila Standard Newspaper, and Manila Bulletin, but acknowledged that 'due to the passing of time, [the petitioner] has lost all copies of [the articles].'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found petitioner failed this criterion for several reasons. First, the petitioner did not show that Woman constitutes a professional or major trade publication or other major media — neither counsel nor petitioner provided objective independent evidence about Woman's reputation, readership, or geographic reach, and 'going on record without supporting documentary evidence is not sufficient' per Matter of Soffici. Second, the plain language of the criterion requires published material in publications (plural), and even if Woman qualified, there was no second qualifying example. Third, regarding the Entrepreneur Magazine, Manila Standard Newspaper, and Manila Bulletin articles, counsel presented no evidence establishing their publication; the CV was self-serving and insufficient, and USCIS need not rely on self-promotional material. The petitioner failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(2) regarding the procedure for documenting unavailability of required evidence or submitting secondary evidence/affidavits. Finally, counsel abandoned reliance on articles other than the Woman article by not continuing to assert them on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel initially asserted at the time of filing (March 11, 2010) that petitioner met this criterion, but provided no continued argument in the August 9, 2010 NOID response or on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concluded that the petitioner abandoned this issue by failing to timely raise it on appeal, citing Sepulveda and Hristov.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed eligibility based on his role as president of multiple international companies and his appointment as international service director of a rotary club. Supporting documents included: (1) petitioner's CV indicating president of three U.S. companies and two Filipino companies; (2) documents for a New York-based U.S. company (certificate of incorporation, IRS letter regarding EIN, online description printout, organizational charts, business plan); (3) documents for a second U.S. company (articles of incorporation, IRS EIN form, 2009-2010 business license, promotional material); (4) documents for a third U.S. company (articles of incorporation, promotional material, May 27, 2010 letter from California's Department of Social Services regarding community care license application, facilities floor plan); and (5) documents for a Filipino company (certificate of incorporation, articles of incorporation, bylaws, general information sheets, petitioner's description of his role, 2006-2008 audited financial statements and tax documents, and a November 5, 2005 award presented to petitioner and the company).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found petitioner failed this criterion. First, although petitioner showed he was or is president of five companies, he submitted no evidence that any of them constitute organizations or establishments with a distinguished reputation. Evidence of conducting business — even successfully in the case of one company — is insufficient to show distinguished reputation. The 'about us' section from the New York-based company stated only an aspirational vision to be a world class organization and 'leader in the field,' which does not suggest an already-distinguished reputation. The 'Mr.' article reflected that one company operates multiple locations, but not every company with multiple branches enjoys a distinguished reputation. Petitioner submitted no objective independent evidence such as articles from professional/trade publications or major media showing any of the five companies' reputation. Second, evidence failed to establish petitioner's role as international service director or board member constituted a leading or critical role for the rotary club — there was insufficient evidence showing what petitioner actually does for the club or what international matters it handles; neither the rotary club documents nor the August 5, 2010 letter shed light on petitioner's duties. Finally, the plain language requires a qualifying role for organizations (plural), so even one qualifying example would be insufficient.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted petitioner's 2009 income was approximately $96,000. Supporting documents included: (1) a 2008 online press release about average Filipino family income; (2) petitioner's 2009 tax-related documents; (3) petitioner's pay slips from July 2009 to January 2010; (4) a Yahoo! Finance online printout indicating petitioner's 2009 income was equivalent to $92,434.17 (from 4,160,000 Philippine Pesos); and (5) a May 28, 2007 online article from The Economic Times regarding CEO salaries.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found petitioner failed this criterion. First, petitioner did not establish the proper U.S. dollar value of his 2009 salary — the Yahoo! Finance printout used the August 5, 2010 exchange rate rather than the 2009 rate, and petitioner provided no evidence regarding the reliability of Yahoo! Finance exchange rate data. Second, the press release related to average Filipino family income and provided no useful comparison for high salaries in petitioner's field, and The Economic Times article from 2007 did not show the 2009 average salary in petitioner's field; petitioner also provided no evidence regarding the article's reliability. Moreover, the article stated that CEOs in the Philippines received an average base salary of $44,496 plus $51,519 in annual cash on average, totaling $96,015 — which is more than petitioner's 2009 salary. Third, even if the article established average CEO salary in the Philippines in 2009, evidence of average wage does not demonstrate what a high wage is in the occupation; merely documenting wages above the average is insufficient under the plain language of the regulation, which requires a high salary in relation to others in the field.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, concurring with the director's finding that petitioner failed to satisfy the antecedent regulatory requirement of presenting at least three of ten types of evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x). Because petitioner failed the initial Kazarian counting step, the AAO stated it need not proceed to a final merits determination, though it noted the evidence was not indicative of a level of expertise consistent with the small percentage at the very top of the field or sustained national or international acclaim. The petitioner also failed to show a one-time achievement of a major internationally recognized award. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, and he did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Sepulveda v. United States Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Soltane v. Dep't of Justice, 381 F.3d 143 (3d Cir. 2004)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Several procedural issues were noted: (1) The April 2010 certificate of membership in the rotary club was unsigned and therefore facially deficient, receiving no evidential weight. (2) Petitioner claimed publication in Entrepreneur Magazine, Manila Standard Newspaper, and Manila Bulletin but admitted losing all copies 'due to the passing of time,' and failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(2) regarding documentation of unavailability of required evidence or submission of secondary evidence/affidavits. (3) The petitioner's CV was deemed self-serving and insufficient self-promotional material. (4) The AAO found petitioner abandoned several arguments (memberships in other organizations, original contributions criterion, and published articles other than the Woman article) by failing to raise them on appeal, citing Sepulveda and Hristov.
